perturbation.original_text: Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) endeavor to hold the Confidential Information in confidence, employing protections it deems appropriate; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for purposes generally aligned with the Permitted Use.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a 'strict confidence' standard, whereas the modified text uses 'endeavor to hold...in confidence' and 'protections it deems appropriate,' making the obligation weaker and more subjective. Replacing 'no purpose other than the Permitted Use' with 'purposes generally aligned with the Permitted Use' introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text's subjective 'protections it deems appropriate' may not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, particularly if those protections are inadequate, thus potentially failing to safeguard trade secrets adequately and violating CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text specifies 'strict confidence' and adherence to a 'reasonable standard of care,' whereas the modified text uses 'endeavor to hold...in confidence' and 'protections it deems appropriate,' which are subjective and potentially less stringent. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'improper means' to include 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the standard of care, the modified text increases the risk of breaching this duty, thus potentially contradicting CUTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall remain liable for any fai lure by its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall be generally responsible for failures by its Representatives to substantially comply with the general intent of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'liable' to 'generally responsible' weakens the accountability. Replacing 'comply with the terms' with 'substantially comply with the general intent' introduces significant ambiguity about the level of compliance required.
perturbation.law_explanation: This code states that 'Before any party to an obligation can require another party to perform any act which such party is entitled to an immediate performance, such party must fulfill all such conditions precedent thereto'. The change undermines the idea that compliance has to be complete, and 'general intent' is not enough.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1439. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. INTERPRETATION OF OBLIGATIONS [1429 - 1451] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Conditional Obligations [1434 - 1442] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1439. Before any party to an obligation can require another party to perform any act under it, he must fulfill all conditions precedent thereto imposed upon himself; and must be able and offer to fulfill all conditions concurrent so imposed upon him on the like fulfillment by the other party, except as provided by the next section. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CHAPTER 3. CONDITIONAL OBLIGATIONS - Sections 1434-1442 :: California Civil Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Civil Code Sections 1434-1442 CHAPTER 3. CONDITIONAL OBLIGATIONS CIVIL CODE SECTION 1434-1442 1434. An obligation is conditional, when the rights or duties of any party thereto depend upon the occurrence of an uncertain event. 1435. Conditions may be precedent, concurrent, or subsequent. 1436. A condition precedent is one which is to be performed before some right dependent thereon accrues, or some act dependent thereon is performed. 1437. Conditions concurrent are those which are mutually dependent, and are to be performed at the same time. 1438. A condition subsequent is one referring to a future event, upon the happening of which the obligation becomes no longer binding upon the other party, if he chooses to avail himself of the condition. 1439. Before any party to an obligation can require another party to perform any act under it, he must fulfill all conditions precedent thereto imposed upon himself; and must be able and offer to fulfill all conditions concurrent so imposed upon him on the like fulfillment by the other party, except as provided by the next section. 1440. If a party to an obligation gives notice to another, before the latter is in default, that he will not perform the same upon his part, and does not retract such notice before the time at which performance upon his part is due, such other party is entitled to enforce the obligation without previously performing or offering to perform any conditions upon his part in favor of the former party. 1441. A condition in a contract, the fulfillment of which is impossible or unlawful, within the meaning of the Article on the Object of Contracts, or which is repugnant to the nature of the interest created by the contract, is void. 1442. A condition involving a forfeiture must be strictly interpreted against the party for whose benefit it is created. Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The original text states that the recipient 'shall remain liable' for failures to comply with the terms, which implies a direct and strict accountability. The changed text weakens this by stating the recipient is 'generally responsible' and requires 'substantial compliance with the general intent'. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 1439, states that a party must fulfill 'all conditions precedent'. By weakening the compliance requirement to 'substantial compliance with the general intent', the modified contract creates ambiguity and undermines the requirement to fulfill all conditions, thus creating a contradiction. Snippet 2 reinforces this by outlining the need for conditions precedent to be met before performance can be required.

perturbation.original_text: It shall not be a breach of this Agreement for the Recipient to disclose Confidential Information if required to do so under law or in a judicial, arbitral, or governmental proceeding or investigation, provided , (i) the Disclosing Party has been given reasonable prior notice to allow i t the option to take actions to protect its interest and Recipient shall cooperate with any reasonable requests of the Disclosing Party in connect ion thereof, including any protective orders or other safeguards sought by the Disclosing Party ; and (ii) the Recipient only discloses that portion of the Confidential Information (with a full copy to the Disclosing Party) required to be disclosed and shall preserve the confidentiality of all other Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may disclose Confidential Information if it believes it is required to do so under law or in a judicial, arbitral, or governmental proceeding or investigation, provided that it attempts to notify the Disclosing Party prior to disclosure, and discloses only what it subjectively deems necessary, while vaguely attempting to maintain some level of confidentiality for the remaining information.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the obligation by changing 'shall not be a breach' to 'may disclose if it believes it is required'. Replacing 'reasonable prior notice' with 'attempts to notify' makes the notification requirement less stringent. Furthermore, replacing 'only discloses that portion...required to be disclosed' with 'discloses only what it subjectively deems necessary' and replacing 'shall preserve the confidentiality' with 'vaguely attempting to maintain some level of confidentiality' introduces subjectivity and reduces the level of protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 2017.010
perturbation.law_explanation: California law has explicit guidance on discovery, particularly when protective orders are in place related to confidential information. By allowing a party to subjectively deem what is 'necessary' and only 'vaguely attempting' confidentiality, the modified agreement could lead to disclosure beyond what a court would compel or allow, especially when proper protective orders could mitigate the need for broad disclosure, thus failing to follow appropriate legal procedure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2017.010 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 2. Scope of Discovery [2017.010 - 2017.320] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) ARTICLE 1. General Provisions [2017.010 - 2017.020] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2017.010. Unless otherwise limited by order of the court in accordance with this title, any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Discovery may relate to the claim or defense of the party seeking discovery or of any other party to the action. Discovery may be obtained of the identity and location of persons having knowledge of any discoverable matter, as well as of the existence, description, nature, custody, condition, and location of any document, electronically stored information, tangible thing, or land or other property. (Amended by Stats. 2012, Ch
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially leading to broader disclosure than legally permissible. The original text requires 'reasonable prior notice' and disclosure of 'that portion...required to be disclosed'. In contrast, the changed text only requires attempting to notify and discloses what is 'subjectively deems necessary,' and 'vaguely attempting' confidentiality. California Code of Civil Procedure CCP 2017.010 allows discovery 'unless otherwise limited by order of the court'. The changes allowing subjective determination and vague attempts at confidentiality directly contradict this, as a court order might specify stricter confidentiality measures than the contract now ensures. The weakening of these obligations, especially concerning confidential information, makes the revised agreement potentially non-compliant with legal standards for discovery and protection of privileged information.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant the Recipient any license, right, title, or interest in or to the Confidential Inform ation.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party generally intends for all Confidential Information to remain its property, but this Agreement does not strictly prohibit the Recipient from potentially claiming some form of interest in the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity regarding ownership. Replacing 'shall remain the exclusive property' with 'generally intends for all Confidential Information to remain its property' weakens the statement. Adding 'but this Agreement does not strictly prohibit the Recipient from potentially claiming some form of interest' further undermines the Disclosing Party's ownership rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: By suggesting the recipient might claim interest in the information, even vaguely, this change may be inconsistent with Trade Secret law where ownership has to be very clear. It suggests a potential challenge to the Disclosing Party's property rights, which creates an ambiguity that might undermine the legal protections normally afforded to trade secrets, potentially creating grounds for litigation and challenging the clear and exclusive ownership expected under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding ownership of confidential information. The original text clearly states that all confidential information 'shall remain the exclusive property' of the disclosing party. The changed text weakens this by stating the disclosing party 'generally intends' for the information to remain its property and does not strictly prohibit the recipient from claiming an interest. This contradicts the intention of trade secret law, where clear ownership is expected. The snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' to include the acquisition of a trade secret 'of another'. By creating ambiguity around who the information belongs to, the changed text undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets, making it more difficult to prevent misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations contained in this Agreement will generally expire approximately five (5) years from the approximate date that Confidential Information was initially disclosed, subject to potential extensions or modifications based on the Disclosing Party's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear 5 year sunset clause on confidentiality. This modification adds ambiguity by adding the word 'generally' to the expiration, 'approximate date' of disclosure, and adding that the expiration is 'subject to potential extensions or modifications based on the Disclosing Party's sole discretion'. This makes the term of the agreement indefinite based on the Disclosing Party's say-so.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section focuses on restricting employment and business activities, the principle of avoiding overly broad restrictions on future conduct is relevant. Making the confidentiality obligations indefinite based on the 'sole discretion' of one party could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it hinders the Recipient's ability to engage in future business activities. California favors competition, and this clause arguably impedes that.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

CONTRACTS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE - Sections 16600-16607 :: California Business and Professions Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Business and Professions Code Sections 16600-16607 CHAPTER 1. CONTRACTS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS CODE SECTION 16600-16607 16600. Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. 16601. Any person who sells the goodwill of a business, or any owner of a business entity selling or otherwise disposing of all of his or her ownership interest in the business entity, or any owner of a business entity that sells (a) all or substantially all of its operating assets together with the goodwill of the business entity, (b) all or substantially all of the operating assets of a division or a subsidiary of the business entity together with the goodwill of that division or subsidiary, or (c) all of the ownership interest of any subsidiary, may agree with the buyer to refrain from carrying on a similar business within a specified geographic area in which the business so sold, or that of the business entity, division, or subsidiary has been carried on, so long as the buyer, or any person deriving title to the goodwill or ownership interest from the buyer, carries on a like business therein. For the purposes of this section, "business entity" means any partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), limited liability company, or corporation. For the purposes of this section, "owner of a business entity" means any partner, in the case of a business entity that is a partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), or any member, in the cas
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The original text had a clear 5-year expiration on confidentiality obligations. The changed text makes the confidentiality obligations indefinite by adding the phrase 'generally expire approximately' and giving the Disclosing Party sole discretion to extend or modify the term, effectively creating a potentially unlimited restraint. While the law snippet focuses on non-compete agreements, the principle of avoiding overly broad restrictions on future conduct is relevant here, because indefinite confidentiality obligations hinder the Recipient's ability to engage in future business activities by limiting the use of information. This aligns with the Law Explanation provided, which states that such a clause could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint.
